title: Signed Numbers and Two's Complement
summary: Two's complement representation, negation, sign extension, and overflow detection.
content_html:
Signed Numbers and Two’s Complement

Two’s complement is the virtually universal method used by modern processors to represent signed integers. It elegantly solves the problems of sign-magnitude representation (two zeros, complicated arithmetic) while enabling the same hardware adder circuit to correctly handle both positive and negative numbers without modification.

The Two’s Complement System

In two’s complement with n bits:

  • Positive numbers (including zero) are represented exactly as in unsigned binary. The most significant bit (MSB) is 0.
  • Negative numbers have the MSB set to 1, but the remaining bits do not simply represent the magnitude.
  • Range: −2n-1 to +2n-1−1. For 8 bits: −128 to +127. For 16 bits: −32,768 to +32,767. For 32 bits: −2,147,483,648 to +2,147,483,647.

Key observation: there is exactly one representation of zero (all bits 0). The range is also slightly asymmetric — one more negative value than positive.

Converting to Two’s Complement

To represent a negative number −X in n-bit two’s complement:

  1. Write the binary representation of +X (the magnitude)
  2. Invert all bits (flip every 0 to 1 and every 1 to 0) — this is the one’s complement
  3. Add 1 to the result

Example: represent −5 in 8-bit two’s complement:

  • +5 in binary: 0000 0101
  • Invert all bits: 1111 1010
  • Add 1: 1111 1011
  • Therefore −5 = 1111 1011 in 8-bit two’s complement

Verification: 1111 1011 in unsigned = 128+64+32+16+8+2+1 = 251 = 256−5. The relationship -X = 2n−X (mod 2n) confirms this.

Converting from Two’s Complement to Decimal

To interpret a two’s complement number:

  • If the MSB is 0: the number is positive; interpret normally as unsigned binary.
  • If the MSB is 1: the number is negative. Either apply the negation algorithm (invert + add 1) to get the magnitude, or use the formula: value = −(MSB × 2n-1) + (remaining bits as unsigned binary).

Example: interpret 1001 0110 (8-bit two’s complement): MSB=1, so negative. Invert: 0110 1001. Add 1: 0110 1010 = 106. So the value is −106.

Arithmetic with Two’s Complement

The beautiful property of two’s complement is that ordinary binary addition works for both positive and negative numbers. The processor uses one adder circuit for all cases:

  • Adding +5 and −3: 0000 0101 + 1111 1101 = (1) 0000 0010. Discard the carry-out; result is 0000 0010 = 2. Correct: 5 + (−3) = 2.
  • Subtraction A−B is performed as A + (−B), and −B is computed using the negation algorithm.

Sign Extension

Sign extension is the process of increasing the bit-width of a two’s complement number while preserving its value. The rule is simple: copy the sign bit (MSB) into all the new higher-order bit positions.

  • Extend +5 from 8-bit to 16-bit: 0000 0101 → 0000 0000 0000 0101 (fill with 0s)
  • Extend −5 (1111 1011) from 8-bit to 16-bit: → 1111 1111 1111 1011 (fill with 1s)

Sign extension is performed automatically by the processor when loading a small value (e.g., a byte) into a larger register (e.g., a 32-bit register). In ARM assembly, LDRSB (Load Register Signed Byte) performs a load with sign extension.

Overflow Detection

Overflow in two’s complement arithmetic occurs when the result falls outside the representable range. The rule for detecting overflow is: overflow occurs when the carry into the sign bit (MSB position) differs from the carry out of the sign bit.

  • Equivalently: if two positive numbers add to give a negative result, or two negative numbers add to give a positive result, overflow has occurred.
  • Hardware detects this condition and sets the V (overflow) flag in the processor status register.
  • Adding numbers of opposite sign can never overflow.

Example: with 8-bit two’s complement, +100 + +100 = 0110 0100 + 0110 0100 = 1100 1000. The MSB is 1, which would mean −56. This is wrong; overflow occurred because 200 > 127.

Comparison of Integer Representations

  • Unsigned: All values non-negative; simple; overflow wraps around; range 0 to 2n−1.
  • Sign-magnitude: Intuitive; two zeros; complicated arithmetic; rarely used today.
  • Two’s complement: Industry standard for signed integers; single zero; arithmetic works uniformly; range −2n-1 to 2n-1−1.

exercises.title: Two's Complement Negation
exercises.prompt: What is the 8-bit two's complement representation of -12?

exercises.title: 8-bit Signed Range
exercises.prompt: What is the range of values representable by an 8-bit two's complement integer?

exercises.title: Sign Extension Rule
exercises.prompt: When sign-extending a negative two's complement number to more bits, what value is used to fill the new high-order bits?

exercises.title: Overflow Detection
exercises.prompt: In 8-bit two's complement, adding +100 and +100 produces which result type?
